title: Heat transfer in fire engineering
text:
Preface
This book is under construction, and can therefore be filled with errors. If you find one, please report is as a bug and we will address it as soon as we can.






Figure 1: Heat on structures

title: 1  Basic concepts
text:
1.1 Excercises on basic concepts of heat transfer
The excersises are created in three levels. The first level consists of exercises to train and automate basic calculations. This level can be revisited at anytime to tune calculational skills. The second level consists of exersises where the skills from the first level needs to connected in order to solve basic fire engineering problems. The third level includes all from the second level but demands more with regards to connecting first- and second level skills. As a complement to these exercises, interactive exercises will be lauched.

title: 1  Basic concepts
text:
1.1.1 First level skills

1.1.1.1 Steady state conduction
A wall consists of three layers according to table 2.1



Table 1.1





\(d_i\) [mm]
Material
\(\lambda_i\) [W/mK]




20
Wood board
0.14


100
Fibre insulation
0.04


20
Gypsum board
0.5






The temperature on the outer face of the wooden board has a constant temperature of 75°C. The opposing side of the wall has a temperature of 15°C, Calculate the temperaturs at the insulations interface surfaces \(T_1\) and \(T_2\).
Solution
By calculating the total thermal resistance of the wall, \(R_{tot}\), it is possible to find intermidiate temperatures using the equation
\[
T_i = T_0 + \frac{\sum_{j = 0}^{i} R_j}{R_{tot}} \left(T_n - T_0\right)
\]
\(R_{tot}\) is the sum of each of the layers as
\[
R_{tot} = R_{wood} + R_{ins} + R_{gyp} = \frac{l_{wood}}{\lambda_{wood}} + \frac{l_{ins}}{\lambda_{ins}} + \frac{l_{gyp}}{\lambda_{gyp}} = \frac{0.02}{0.14} + \frac{0.1}{0.04} + \frac{0.12}{0.5} = 2.67 \text{ } m²K/W
\]
As \(T_0\) = 75°C and \(T_n\) = 15°C, the temperatures at each layer interface, \(T_1\) and \(T_2\) can be calculated as
\[
T_1 = T_0 + \frac{R_{wood}}{R_{tot}} \left(T_n - T_0 \right) =
75 + \frac{\tfrac{0.02}{0.14}}{2.67} \left(15 - 75 \right) = 71.8°C
\]
and
\[
T_2 = T_0 + \frac{R_{wood} + R_{ins}}{R_{tot}} \left(T_n - T_0 \right) =
75 + \frac{\tfrac{0.02}{0.14} + \tfrac{0.1}{0.04}}{2.67} \left(15 - 75 \right) = 15.6°C
\]


1.1.1.2 Surface temperature
Assume the heat transfer coefficient, \(h_{tot}\), equal to 5 W/m²K for the exposed side and a constant temperature on the unexposed side, \(T_2\), equal to 20°C. Calculate the surface temperature, \(T_1\).



Table 1.2





\(d_i\) [mm]
Material
\(\lambda_i\) [W/mK]




12
Wood board
0.2






Solution
By calculating the total thermal resistance of the wall, \(R_{tot}\), it is possible to find intermidiate temperatures using the equation
\[
T_i = T_0 + \frac{\sum_{j = 0}^{i} R_j}{R_{tot}} \left(T_n - T_0\right)
\]
In this case, \(T_0\) = \(T_g\), \(T_n\) = \(T_2\), and \(R_{tot}\) is the total resistance of the wall including the thermal resistance of the exposed side.
\[
R_{tot} = R_{h} + R_{k} = \frac{1}{h_{tot}} + \frac{l_{wood}}{\lambda_{wood}} = \frac{1}{5} + \frac{0.02}{0.14} = 0.26 \text{ } m²K/W
\]
As \(T_g\) = 100°C and \(T_2\) = 20°C, the temperatures at the surface, \(T_1\), can be calculated as
\[
T_1 = T_g + \frac{R_{h}}{R_{tot}} \left(T_2 - T_g \right) =
75 + \frac{\tfrac{1}{5}}{0.26} \left(20 - 100 \right) = 38.5°C
\]
An alternative way to calculate the surface temperature is by using
\[
T_1 = \frac{R_h T_2 + R_k T_g}{R_{h} + R_k} = 38.5°C
\]
Both solutions yield the same result and are proper to use.


1.1.1.3 Cylinder temperature
Assume an insulated steel pipe with a liquid medium of the temperature, \(T_{in} = 100°C\). The pipe is exposed from the outside by a temperature, \(T_f\) = 800°C. The inner heat transfer coefficient, \(h_{in}\) = 100 W/m²K for the exposed outside, \(h_{out}\) = 50 W/m²K. The conductivity of steel, \(\lambda_s\) = 45 W/mK and the conductivity of the insulation, \(\lambda_i\) = 0.5 W/mK. Calculate the temperature of the steel pipe, \(T_1\).

Inner radius, \(r_i\) = 28 mm
Steel thickness, \(d_s\) = 2 mm
Insulation thickness, \(d_i\) = 50 mm

Solution
By calculating the total thermal resistance of the wall, \(R_{tot}\), it is possible to find intermidiate temperatures using the equation
\[
T_i = T_0 + \frac{\sum_{j = 0}^{i} R_j}{R_{tot}} \left(T_n - T_0\right)
\]
In this case, \(T_0\) = \(T_in\), \(T_n\) = \(T_f\), and \(R_{tot}\) is the total resistance of the pipe including the thermal resistance of both sides.
\[
R_{tot} = R_{in} + R_{s} + R_{i} + R_{out} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\left(\frac{1}{r_i h_{in}} + \frac{\ln\left(r_{o,s}/r_{i,s}\right)}{\lambda_{s}} + \frac{\ln\left(r_{o,i}/r_{i,i}\right)}{\lambda_{i}} + \frac{1}{r_{o,i} h_{out}}\right) \approx 0.41 \text{ } m²K/W
\]
As \(T_{in}\) = 100°C and \(T_f\) = 800°C, the temperatures at the surface, \(T_1\), can be calculated as
\[
T_1 = T_{in} + \frac{R_{in}}{R_{tot}} \left(T_f - T_{in} \right) =
100 + \frac{\tfrac{1}{2\pi 0.0028 \cdot 100}}{0.41} \left(800 - 100 \right) = 197.3°C
\]
An alternative way to calculate the surface temperature is by using
\[
T_1 = \frac{R_{in} T_2 + \left(R_s + R_i + R_{out}\right) T_g}{R_{tot}} = 197.3°C
\]
Both solutions yield the same result and are proper to use.

title: 2  Conduction
text:
2.1.1 Steel plate thermal constant
A 2 mm steel plate with an initial tempeprature, \(T_i\) = 20°C is suddelnly exposed on both sides to a gas temperature, \(T_f\) = 500°C. Assume a constant heat transfer coefficent, \(h\) = 20 W/m²K and the steel properties \(c\) = 460 J/kgK and \(\rho\) = 7850 kg/m³.

Calculate the thermal time constant of the steel plate.
Calculate the temperature of the steel plate after 5 min.

Solution

The thermal time constant is calculated according to

\[
\tau = \frac{dc\rho}{h} = \frac{\tfrac{0.002}{2}460\cdot 7850}{20} = 181 s
\]
note that the thickness, \(d\), is divided by half as the plate is exposed on both sides.

At time \(t\) = 300 , the steel temperature is then calculated as

\[
T_s = T_i + \left( T_f - T_i \right) \left( 1 - e^{-\tfrac{t}{\tau}} \right) = 20 + \left( 500 - 20 \right)\left( 1 - e^{-\tfrac{300}{181}} \right) = 409°C
\]


2.1.2 Steel bulkhead
A 5 mm steel bulkhead is suddenly exposed to a fire. It is insulated on the fire-exposed side and unisulated on the unexposed side.
Boundary conditions on the fire-exposed side * Temperature, \(T_f\) = 1000°C * Heat transfer coefficient negligable (assume \(T_s\) = \(T_f\))



Table 2.1












\(d_i\) [mm]
Material
\(\lambda\) [W/mK]
\(c\) [J/kgK]
\(\rho\) [kg/m³]




100
Insulation
0.07
-
-


5
Steel
0.04
460
7850






Boundary conditions on the unexposed side * Temperature, \(T_\infty\) = 20°C * Heat transfer coefficient \(h\) = 5 W/m²K
What is the ultimate steel temperature, \(T_s^{ult}\)?
Solution

\(T_s^{ult}\) can be found when the temperature change is zero. This occurs when the heat entering the steel equal the heat exiting.

The heat entering the steel can be described as the heat passing through the insulation,
\[
\dot{q}_{in}^{''}=\frac{\lambda_s}{d_s}\left(T_f - T_s\right)
\]
The heat exiting the steel can be described as
\[
\dot{q}_{out}^{''}=h\left(T_{\infty} - T_s)
\]
These two are equal when \(T_s\) = \(T_s^{ult}\). This can be found by using
\[
T_s^{ult}=\frac{\tfrac{k}{d}T_f + hT_{\infty}}{\tfrac{k}{f} + h} =
\frac{\tfrac{0.07}{0.1}1000 + 5\cdot 20}{\tfrac{0.07}{0.1} + 5} = 140°C
\]


2.1.3 Thermal penetration depth
The surface temperature of a thick concrete wall with an initial temperature of 0°C rises suddely to 1000°C.

What is the 1 % thermal penetration depth, \(\delta_{0.01}\), after 15 minutes?
What is the temperature, \(T\) at that point after 60 minutes?

Assume the thermal properties of the concrete to be

\(c\) = 900 J/kgK
\(\rho\) = 2300 kg/m³
\(\lambda\) = 1.5 W/mK

Solution

By calculating

\[
\alpha = \frac{\lambda}{c \rho} = \frac{1.5}{900 \cdot 2300} = 7.25 \cdot 10^{-7}
\]
The penetration depth after 15 minutes (900 s) can be calculated using
\[
\delta_{0.01} = 3.6 \sqrt{\alpha t} = 3.6 \sqrt{7.25 \cdot 10^{-7} \cdot 900} = 0.092 m
\]

The temperature after 60 minutes at the depth \(x\) = \(\delta_{0.01}\) is calcualted using

\[
\frac{T - T_i}{T_s - T_s} = erfc\left(\eta \right)
\]
\(\eta\) as
\[
\eta = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}} = \frac{0.092}{2 \sqrt{7.25 \cdot 10^{-7} \cdot 3600}} = 0.90
\]
The temperature rise can be calculated by rearranging the first equation for the relative temperature rise in semi-infinite solids with a sudden rise in surface temperature to
\[
T = T_i + erfc\left(\eta\right)\left(T_s - T_i \right) = 0 + erfc\left(0.90\right)\left(1000 - 0\right) = 202.8°C
\]
